themes.heading: Safety
themes.text: Violent and petty crime is not common in Dili, however, the risk increases at night and if travelling alone, in some parts of the city. Criminals sometimes target foreigners. Home invasions, while not frequent, do occur. Lock your accommodation, even when you're inside. Drive with doors locked and windows up. Protests may occur. Avoid protests and large gatherings, including around Tasi Tolu and Comoro in Dili. Transport and other essential services may be disrupted. Local unrest occurs, including street gang fighting and political demonstrations. Minor disputes can become violent. Be alert to the potential risks around drink spiking and methanol poisoning through consuming alcoholic drinks. Don't leave drinks unattended. See our advice on Partying safely while overseas. Travellers can experience sexual harassment, including lewd remarks and indecent exposure. Although offenders mostly target women, they may harass men. Sexual assault can happen in public places, such as Beach Road in Dili. Travel as a group if you can, including when exercising outdoors. Don't use public transport or taxis alone, especially at night. Crocodile attacks are reported in the eastern districts and along the southern coast. Crocodiles have been seen on beaches and inland waterways, including in Dili. Follow local advice. Severe storms occur during the wet season, usually from November to April, which can last longer. Flooding and landslides can damage roads and disrupt services. Follow the advice of local authorities. Timor-Leste is in an active seismic zone. Earthquakes do occur and there is a risk of tsunamis. Familiarise yourself with earthquake security measures in hotels and public and private buildings. In the event of an earthquake, pay careful attention to the advice of local authorities. Full travel advice: Safety

themes.heading: Health
themes.text: Rabies has been detected in Timor-Leste and neighbouring West Timor (Indonesia). Rabies is fatal without post-exposure treatment, recommended within 24 hours of exposure. Avoid dogs, cats, monkeys, bats and other mammals. In the event of an animal bite or scratch, seek urgent medical advice. Rabies post-exposure treatment includes vaccinations and, if necessary, immunoglobulin treatment. This could require transportation to Australia as access to post-exposure rabies vaccination in Timor-Leste is limited. While malaria cases are uncommon, other mosquito-borne diseases can be found in Timor-Leste. Dengue is endemic in Timor-Leste, peaking in the wet season (usually from November to April). Check your accommodation is insect-proof and use insect repellent. Waterborne, foodborne and other infectious diseases are widespread. These include gastroenteritis, typhoid, tuberculosis and hepatitis. Drink only boiled or bottled water. Avoid raw or undercooked food. Tuberculosis is widespread. Healthcare workers are at risk. If you're a healthcare worker, get tested for tuberculosis before you travel and after you leave. Fish sold on the roadside may be contaminated with formaldehyde. Follow local warnings and advice. Medical and dental facilities are limited, including at Dili National Hospital, which treats most emergency cases. If you're seriously ill or injured, you'll need medical evacuation. Check your travel insurance covers this. Full travel advice: Health

themes.heading: Local laws
themes.text: Don't use or carry illegal drugs. There are serious penalties for drug offences. Don't interfere in local political processes or take part in political activity. If you do, you may face fines, arrest, detention or deportation. Timor-Leste doesn't recognise de facto or same-sex relationships. Social and cultural attitudes towards same-sex relationships are conservative. Avoid public displays of affection. Dress and behaviour standards are conservative. Don't wear revealing clothing in public places, churches or markets. Public displays of affection aren't socially acceptable. Loud noise and foolish behaviour on religious days are offensive. Full travel advice: Local laws

themes.heading: Travel
themes.text: You can get a 30-day visa on arrival if you visit as a tourist and arrive via President Nicolau Lobato International Airport or Dili Seaport. If you enter at another location or by land from West Timor (Indonesia), you'll need to get a visa before arrival. Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice. Contact the nearest Timor-Leste Embassy or Consulate for the latest information. Many taxis are in poor condition. You're at risk of robbery, sexual harassment, and sexual assault in taxis. Avoid using taxis, especially if you're female. If you choose to use taxis, seek local advice on recommended companies. Airport infrastructure across the country may be maintained to a different standard than in Australia, including emergency response vehicles. Full travel advice: Travel

themes.heading: Local contacts
themes.text: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance. There are limits to what we can do. Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas. For consular help, contact the Australian Embassy in Dili. To stay up to date with local information, follow the Embassy's social media accounts. Full travel advice: Local contacts

themes.heading: Demonstrations and protests
themes.text: Protests may occur. Avoid protests and large gatherings. Public protests and events that draw large groups of people can turn violent. Transport and other essential services may be disrupted. Check media for updates and follow the advice of local authorities. Timor-Leste’s security situation has been increasingly stable and benign for several years. People have occasionally protested against the Australian Government outside the Australian Embassy. They could protest again. Australians have been harassed because of their nationality. To stay safe: pay attention to your personal safety avoid unplanned gatherings, demonstrations, protests, street rallies and large public gatherings as they may turn violent check media for news about safety and security risks avoid identified trouble spots Move to a safe place if: you're being harassed gatherings turn violent In Dili, be careful around Tasi Tolu, Comoro and other crowded areas, and at night in areas which attract crowds.

themes.heading: Civil unrest and political tension
themes.text: There is a history of gang-related violence, robbery, arson and vandalism in major towns, especially Dili. Minor disputes can escalate into violence without warning. Martial Arts Groups (MAGs) and Ritual Arts Groups (RAGs) are mass membership organisations with a complex history in Timorese society and political culture. On 10 November 2023, the Timorese government announced a ban on all MAG and RAG activities until 31 December 2025. Rival groups are frequently involved in violence against each other, which increases the potential for local disputes between individuals or families to escalate into communal violence. More information: Protests and civil unrest

themes.heading: Crime
themes.text: Criminals sometimes target foreigners for petty crimes such as theft. Although uncommon, there have been attacks in Dili, nearby beaches, and rural areas. Home and room invasions occur. Lock your accommodation, including when you're inside. Crime risks increase at night, and if you're travelling alone. Smash-and-grab property theft from vehicles happens. Intruders have broken into homes where foreigners are known to be. Gang-related violence, robbery, arson and vandalism occur in major towns, including Dili. Rocks are sometimes thrown at vehicles and property. This mostly happens during the early evening and at night. To stay safe: be alert to your surroundings don't carry bags that are easy to snatch keep your car windows up and doors locked, even when moving don't leave valuables exposed in your car secure your accommodation, even when you're home

themes.heading: Sexual harassment and assault
themes.text: Sexual harassment of foreigners is common. This usually affects women. However, men can also be targeted. Harassment can include: lewd remarks indecent exposure indecent touching and groping Violence against local women is widespread. Sexual assault can happen at any time. People have reported assaults in areas in Dili such as: Beach Road Beaches around Cristo Rei (Jesus) Statue beach area and Back Beach area, behind the Jesus Statue Tasi Tolu To stay safe: travel as a group, including when exercising outdoors don't visit isolated areas alone don't travel alone on public transport or in taxis, especially at night

themes.heading: Kidnapping
themes.text: Kidnapping can happen anywhere, even in places that seem safe. The Australian Government’s longstanding policy is that it doesn’t make payments or concessions to kidnappers. More information, including advice on how to reduce your risk of kidnapping: Kidnapping

themes.heading: Terrorism
themes.text: Terrorism is a threat worldwide. Terrorist attacks could occur anywhere and at any time. Be alert to possible threats, especially in public places. Terrorists may target tourist areas or other places frequented by foreigners and where large crowds may gather. If there’s a terrorist incident, leave the area if it’s safe to do so and follow the instructions of local authorities. More information: Terrorism

themes.heading: Extreme weather and natural disasters
themes.text: Timor-Leste experiences cyclones, earthquakes, tsunamis, bushfires and severe weather. Severe storms usually happen during the wet season, from November to April. Heavy rain can cause flooding, landslides, damage to roads and disruptions to services. If a natural disaster or severe weather happens: keep your passport in a safe, waterproof location check local media and other sources follow the advice of local authorities keep in touch with friends and family Stay up to date on weather conditions and forecasts, natural disaster watches and warnings. Plan accordingly. If you're travelling during the wet season or after a natural disaster, ask your tour operator if services are affected. More information: Australian Bureau of Meteorology Global Disaster Alert and Coordination System World Meteorological Organisation Severe Weather Information Centre

themes.heading: Earthquakes and tsunamis
themes.text: Earthquakes and tsunamis happen. For real-time information on earthquakes, see the US Geological Service. To receive tsunami alerts, register with the US Tsunami Warning Center. Follow the advice of your accommodation provider and local authorities. If you're near the coast, move to high ground immediately if advised, or if you: feel a strong earthquake that makes it hard to stand up feel a weak, rolling earthquake that lasts a minute or more see a sudden rise or fall in sea level hear loud and unusual noises from the sea Don't wait for official warnings such as alarms or sirens. Once on high ground, check local media.

themes.heading: Travel insurance
themes.text: Get comprehensive travel insurance before you leave and closely read the Product Disclosure Statement (PDS) for what is and isn’t covered. It must cover all medical costs overseas, including evacuation. The Australian Government will not pay for these costs. If you can’t afford travel insurance, you shouldn’t travel. This is true for everyone, even if you are healthy and fit. If you don’t have insurance, you might have to pay thousands of dollars up front for medical care. Before you travel, check your policy, make sure: you know what activities and medical care your insurance covers, including evacuations your insurance covers you for your whole trip, including any stopovers you know any exclusions – for example, you might need to pay extra for activities like motorcycling, snow sports, scuba diving or bungee jumping. More information: Travel insurance

themes.heading: Physical and mental health
themes.text: Travelling overseas can be stressful and uncertain. You may experience unfamiliar environments, travel disruptions, changes in normal routines and limited access to health care. Not taking your prescribed medications is a common cause of mental ill-health for Australians overseas. If you are concerned about your welfare, or someone else's, contact your nearest Australian embassy, high commission, or consulate. You can also call the 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre on +61 2 6261 3305. Mental health resources: Information and advice on mental illness (HealthDirect). Living with mental illness (BeyondBlue) Medicare Mental Health (the Department of Health and Aged Care) More information: General health advice Mental health support overseas

themes.heading: Mosquito-borne diseases
themes.text: Insect borne diseases are common in Timor-Leste, including: malaria dengue Ask your doctor about health risks and vaccines before you go. More information: Infectious diseases Other health risks rabies gastroenteritis typhoid hepatitis tuberculosis Fish poisoning Local authorities have advised that fish sold on the roadside can be contaminated with formaldehyde. Follow local warnings and advice.

themes.heading: Medical care
themes.text: There’s no agreement between Australia and Timor-Leste for free healthcare. Tourists must pay for all medical care, even if they need to stay in hospital or be evacuated. This can be very expensive. Make sure your travel insurance covers you. The Australian Government won't pay for these costs. Medical and dental facilities are limited. There are some private clinics such as Stamford Medical Clinic, Dili Medical Centre and Flodova Clinic in Dili. Most emergency cases are treated at Dili National Hospital (HGNV), which has limited facilities. A limited supply of basic medications is available. There is no hyperbaric chamber in Timor-Leste. If you're going to dive, make sure you have a current medical clearance and you're covered by insurance. If you become seriously ill or injured, you'll need to be evacuated. Medical evacuation can be very expensive. The closest medevac location is Darwin, Australia (approximately 1.5 hours from Dili). Make sure you have appropriate insurance.

themes.heading: Drugs
themes.text: There are serious penalties for drug offences. More information: Carrying or using drugs

themes.heading: LGBTQIA+ travellers
themes.text: Timor-Leste doesn't recognise de facto or same-sex relationships. Social and cultural attitudes towards same-sex relationships are conservative. Avoid public displays of affection. They aren't socially acceptable. More information: LGBTQIA+ travellers

themes.heading: Local customs
themes.text: Timor-Leste has conservative standards of behaviour and dress. Dress modestly in public places, churches or markets. Take care not to offend. If in doubt, seek local advice. Religious days are closely observed. Show respect to local customs.

themes.heading: Visas and border measures
themes.text: Each country or territory decides its own entry and exit rules, including health border screening measures. To find out what you need to enter a destination, ask their embassy, consulate or immigration office.

themes.heading: Visas
themes.text: You can get a 30-day visa when you arrive, if you're both: visiting as a tourist, and arriving via President Nicolau Lobato International Airport or Dili Seaport You have to pay for a visa on arrival with cash in US dollars. There are no money exchange facilities at the airport. Debit or credit cards are not accepted. If you enter at another location including by land from West Timor (Indonesia), you need to get a visa before you arrive. You may not be allowed to travel to Timor-Leste if your stay is booked for longer than 30 days and you don’t have a visa before your travel. To extend your visa, apply at the Immigration Department at Vila Verde in Dili to extend your visa. You must give a valid reason to immigration authorities. Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice. Contact the nearest embassy or consulate or Timor-Leste Immigration for details about visas, currency, customs and quarantine rules.

themes.heading: Lost or stolen passport
themes.text: Your passport is valuable and can be used by others to commit crimes. Don’t let anyone trick you into giving it to them. Always keep it in a safe place. If your passport is lost or stolen, tell the Australian Government: In Australia: contact the Australian Passport Information Service. Overseas: contact the nearest Australian embassy or consulate.

themes.heading: Passport with 'X' gender identifier
themes.text: Australian passports comply with international standards for sex and gender. If your passport has an 'X' in the sex field, we can’t guarantee your entry or transit to other countries. Ask the embassy or consulate of your destinations if they’ll accept your passport with an 'X' gender marker. Check all transit destinations too. More information: LGBTQIA+ travellers

themes.heading: Money
themes.text: The official currency is the US Dollar (USD). You should enter Timor-Leste with enough cash in USD for your entire stay. You might not be able to change Australian dollars and cards are not widely accepted for transactions. Most places won't accept credit/debit cards, including visas on arrival, hotels, shops and restaurants – you must pay in cash in USD. Visa cards and Union Pay are the only two card types accepted in Timor-Leste, including for ATM withdrawals. ATMs are only available in built-up areas and may not always be restocked. Ask your bank if your Australian credit or debit cards will work in Timor-Leste. Declare all amounts higher than $US5,000 or equivalent when you arrive and depart. This covers all forms of currency, not only cash. You need approval from the Central Bank of Timor-Leste (CBTL) to import or export more than $US10,000. It's illegal to import currencies other than USD, including AUD, above certain limits without a permit from the CBTL. If you import more than $US2,000 worth of Australian dollars in a calendar month, you will need approval. Contact the Central Bank of Timor-Leste for details: email: info@bancocentral.tl call: CBTL +670 331 371 2 / 4 / 5 / 8 US banknotes issued before 2000 are not accepted.

themes.heading: Driving permit
themes.text: To drive in Timor-Leste, you need both: a valid International Driving Permit (IDP) your current Australian driver's licence (digital driver's licences may not be accepted) Get your IDP before you leave Australia. Your travel and vehicle insurance might not be valid if you don't have an IDP. After three months, you'll need to get a local licence.

themes.heading: Road travel
themes.text: Driving conditions are dangerous because of: bad roads heavy traffic poor signage a lack of street lighting In rural areas, dangers include: sinking ground large potholes pedestrians and animals on the road vehicles without headlights Large crowds can form quickly after traffic accidents. The situation can become violent with little warning. Main routes are often single-lane mountain roads. They can worsen quickly and become blocked. This can happen more readily in the rainy season from November to April. Outside Dili, Australian officials travelling for work don't drive at night other than in exceptional situations. There are limited emergency response resources. Check security and road conditions with local authorities before you travel. Make sure you have adequate insurance cover before driving. If you drive: learn local traffic laws and driving practices carry your car registration papers and driver's licence keep car windows up and doors locked travel in convoy in regional areas follow the advice of local authorities If you're in an accident, report it to the local police. More information: Driving or riding

themes.heading: Motorcycles
themes.text: Check if your travel insurance policy covers you when riding a motorbike. Always wear a helmet.

themes.heading: Taxis
themes.text: Many taxis are in poor condition. Driving standards are often poor. If you choose to use taxis, seek local advice on recommended companies. You're at risk of robbery, sexual harassment and sexual assault in a taxi. It's safer to arrange private transfers or hire cars through your hotel. Avoid using taxis, especially if you're a woman.

themes.heading: Public transport
themes.text: Many buses, 'mikrolets' and trucks used as public transport are not well maintained. Poor driving standards add to the risk.

themes.heading: Sea travel
themes.text: Travel by boat can be dangerous. Not all vessels are seaworthy. Passenger limits are sometimes exceeded. Don't get on or stay on an overloaded vessel. Before booking sea travel, confirm that vessel maintenance and safety standards are maintained. There may not be enough life jackets on board. Make sure any vessel you board is carrying safety equipment, including enough life jackets for all crew, passengers and children. Some cruise lines have stopped over in Timor-Leste. More information: Going on a cruise Travelling by boat

themes.heading: Piracy
themes.text: Piracy happens in South-East Asian waters. The International Maritime Bureau publishes piracy reports. Avoid trouble spots and take safety precautions.

themes.heading: Air travel
themes.text: DFAT doesn’t give advice about how safe different airlines or flight paths are. Safety risks and weather events may cause flight delays and cancellations. Contact your travel agent or airline to confirm your plans. Airlines and airports can restrict what you can take in your luggage. Regulations may be different to those in Australia. Check with your airline before you travel. Check Timor-Leste's air safety profile with the Aviation Safety Network. Airport infrastructure across the country may be maintained to a different standard than in Australia, including emergency response vehicles. More information: Air travel

themes.heading: Consular contacts
themes.text: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance. There are limits to what we can do. Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas. For consular assistance, contact the Australian Embassy in Dili.

themes.heading: Australian Embassy, Dili
themes.text: Rua Martires da Patria Dili Timor-Leste Phone: (+67 0) 3322 111 Website: timorleste.embassy.gov.au Email: austemb_dili@dfat.gov.au Facebook: Australia in Timor-Leste X: @AusAmbDili Check the Embassy website for details about opening hours and any temporary closures.

themes.heading: 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre
themes.text: In a consular emergency, if you can't contact an embassy, call the 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre on: +61 2 6261 3305 from overseas 1300 555 135 in Australia

themes.heading: 사건ㆍ사고 현황
themes.text: ㅇ 주재국 정부의 공식 통계발표는 없습니다. 무술단체간 세력다툼이나 일부 반정부무장단체에 의한 치안이 불안한 일부지역(특히 바우카우지역)으로 이동시 주의가 특히 요구됩니다. 기타 절도, 강도, 돌멩이 투척 등의 사건사고가 지속적으로 발생하고 있습니다. ㅇ 2014년 12월말부터 2015년 1월간 주재국 무술단체(Martial Arts Group, MAG)간 세력타툼으로 3명이 사망하고 집 7채가 전소되는 사건이 발생하였습니다. - 상기 무술단체(대부분 PSHT, IKS 등 인도네시아에서 들어온 것들임)들은 2006-2008년간 발생한 주재국 소요사태와도 관련되어 있는 조직으로 지난 2003년 주재국 정부에 의해 불법화 된 단체임에도 불구하고 상기과 같은 사건으로 동 단체들이 여전히주재국내에서 활동하고 있음을 보여주는 사례입니다. - 주재국 경찰 및 군내조직에도 상기 상당수 무술조직원들이 활동하고 있음을 볼 때 2017년 주재국 총선 및 대선에도 일부 영향을 미칠 가능성도 배제할 수 없습니다. ㅇ 2015년 3월부터 시작된 주재국 군경합동치안부대에 의한 주재국 반정부 무장단체인 마우베레혁명평의회( 의장 : 마욱모룩) 추종자 검거 작전이 본격화 된 이후 전반적으로 주재국내 일부지역(바우카우주) 치안상황은 불안정한 상황입니다. ㅇ 주재국 군경합동팀과 반정부단체인 KRM과의 총격전으로 마침내 2015.8.8 마욱모룩이 사망함에 따라 장기적으로는 주재국 치안상황은 나아질 것으로 추정되나 마우모룩을 추종하는 세력들에 의한 국지적 소요사태가 발생할 가능성도 있습니다.

themes.heading: 사건ㆍ사고의 유형
themes.text: [우리 국민들에게 주로 발생하는사건] ㅇ 우리 국민들에 대한 살인, 강도 등 강력범죄는현재까지 발생하고 있지 않으나 연말 연초나 혹은 이사할 즈음 집에 보관한 귀중품들이 도난 당하는 경우가 발생하였습니다. ㅇ 또한, 차에 물건 등을 두고 내리는 경우 차 창문을 부수고 차량내 물건을 훔쳐가는 경우도 있으며 특히 차량이동시 현지인이 오토바이 등을 타고 차를 뒤좇으면서 동 차량에 의해 피해를 입었다고 하면서 돈을 요구하는 경우로 신고하는 경우가 있습니다. ㅇ 여성들의 경우 혼자서 이동하거나 택시 등 대중교통을 이용하는 경우에도 현지인들에 의해 성추행 등을 당하는 경우도 있습니다. ㅇ 무엇보다도 주재국에서 마약관련 활동에 연루될 경우 즉시 체포되어 상당히 중하게 처벌됨에 따라 주의가 요구됩니다. ㅇ 특히 주재국에서는 사람들이 많이 모인 경우 예기치 않게 폭력으로 변하는 경우가 있어 그런 경우 재빨리 그 자리에서 벗어나는 것이 필요합니다. ㅇ 그 밖에 당지에서 활동중인 외국인들이 겪는 하기와 같은 일반범죄에도 노출이 되는 경우도 많이 있습니다. [일반사건/사고 유형] ㅇ 12.12월 유엔경찰 철수 이후부터는 동티모르 경찰이 치안을 담당하고 있습니다. ㅇ 수도인 딜리(Dili)(인구: 약 20만명)에서는 절도, 강도, 날치기 등 범죄가 지속 발생되고 있으며, 지방의 치안상황은 좀 더 취약합니다. ㅇ 야간에는 갱단등 범죄 집단의 활동이 목격되며, 택시기사들이 승객을 대상으로 강도 행위를 벌이는 경우도 종종 발생하고 있습니다. ㅇ 오토바이를 이용하여 은행이나 슈퍼마켓 근처에서 나오는 외국인을 대상으로 핸드백을 낚아 채 달아나는 소매치기 범죄도 있으니 유의하셔야 합니다. ㅇ 택시 요금을 고액권으로 지불 시 잔돈이 없다는 이유로 거스름돈을 돌려주지 않는 사례가 많으니 택시 이용 시에는 1불짜리 지폐와 동전을 미리 준비하는 것이 좋습니다. 또한 택시는 미터기가 없어 탑승전 미리 요금을 협상하여 결정하는 것이 좋습니다. ㅇ 주정차된 차량의 유리를 깨고 지갑, 가방 등을 탈취해 가는 범죄도 있으니 개인 차량 이용 시 차량 내에 중요한 물품 등을 남기지 마시기 바랍니다. ㅇ 범죄의 주원인이 빈곤, 실업 등 생계문제와 관련된 것들인 만큼 화려한 복장이나 장신구 착용, 과다한 현금 소지 등은 피하여 주시고, 주야를 막론하고 외진 곳의 출입을 자제해야 합니다. 외출 시는 가급적 3명 이상 단체로 다니는 것이 보다 안전합니다. ㅇ 한국인에 대한 이미지는 좋으나, 아시아인들에 대한 야유, 시비걸기 등도 발생하고 있습니다. 특히 외국인 거주 시설이나 주택에 동네 청년들이 돌멩이를 투척하는 경우도 있으므로 유의해야 합니다. ㅇ 주재국 이동시 안전을 위해 현지인을 동행하고 다니는 것이 좋습니다. 외국인이 보험에 가입되지 않은 차량으로 사고를 유발할 경우 사고 지역의 불량배들을 중심으로 주민들이 폭력적으로 돌발, 고액의 보상을 요구하는 경우가 많습니다. 사고가 발생하면 순식간에 수십 명의 현지인들이 주위에 몰려들기 때문에 휴대폰을 통해 경찰(112) 및 대사관에 도움을 청하는 등 지혜롭게 대처하고 현장을 최대한 신속히 대피하는 것이 현명합니다. ㅇ 도로에 염소, 돼지, 닭, 개 등 가축들이 활보하는 경우가 많고, 우기시 토사가 무너져 도로가로 덮치는 경우가 많음으로 운전 시 특히 주의를 요합니다. 운전 중 가축을 치었을 경우, 주인에게 적절한 보상을 한 후 현장을 신속히 떠나는 것이 바람직합니다. ㅇ 도로 가로등이 취약하여 야간 운전은 가시거리가 짧아 유의하여야 합니다. 지방을 여행하거나 야간 운전 시 돌멩이를 던지는 경우가 있으므로 주의를 요합니다. 주민들 가운데는 외국인들에게 특별한 이유나 감정이 없이 습관적으로 돌멩이를 던지는 경향이 있습니다. ㅇ 출퇴근 시간 등 번잡한 시간을 이용하여 외국인이 운전하는 차량에 고의로 오토바이 접촉사고를 일으켜 금전을 요구하는 경우도 간혹 있으니 직접 운전 시 오토바이를 주의하시기 바랍니다.

themes.heading: 자연재해
themes.text: ㅇ 동티모르 인근 해안지역에 지진이 발생하는 경우가 관측되고 있습니다. ㅇ 2021년 4월, 최근 40년 이내 최악의 홍수 피해로 36명 사망자, 10명 실종자 발생(우기) ㅇ 열대성의 소나기가 쏟아질 때는 낙뢰를 동반하는 경우가 많으니 차량운전시 특히 주의가 요구되며 가급적 외출을 자제하는 것이 좋습니다. ㅇ 배수시설, 저수지, 댐 등의 부재로 집중 호우 발생 시 도로, 강, 계곡이 일순간에 범람하며, 산사태는 물론, 불어난 물살에 사람들이 실종되는 사고가 빈발하므로, 우기(11월~5월)시 여행은 최대한 자제하고, 여행 중 호우를 만나는 경우 신속히 높은 지대로 안전하게 대피하는 것이 좋습니다. ㅇ 또한 우기에는 지방의 도로가 유실되거나 산사태로 막히는 경우가 빈번한바, 지방 여행시는 도로 상황을 사전에 점검해야 합니다.

themes.heading: 유의해야할 지역
themes.text: ㅇ 주재국 바우카우 지역(특히, 라가(Laga), 바기아(Bagia)) 및 라우템주 일부 지역(특히 Luro)에는 반정부 무장단체가 활동하는 지역으로 이동시 주의가 요구됩니다. ㅇ 수도 딜리를 여행시 큰 도로 등을 이용하고, 마을 안쪽이나 이면 도로는 가급적 피하여야 합니다. 동티모르는 부족, 마을, 촌락 공동체 성향이 강하므로, 외딴 마을에 방문하시는 경우 주의 하셔야 합니다. 특히, 딜리 베보눅(Bebonuk)과 베토(Beto) 등 일부 지역은 범죄 발생이 빈번하므로 유의하셔야 합니다. ㅇ 우기에 지방을 여행할 경우 불량배들이 “파손된 도로를 수리했다거나, 교통 장애물을 제거했다.” 면서 지나가는 차량을 세운 뒤 도끼와 칼 등으로 위협하면서 통행세(약 5~10불 내외)를 강요하는 사례도 있습니다. 한적한 산이나 외딴 곳에서 발생되므로, 이런 상황에서는 저항하지 않는 것이 더 큰 피해를 예방할 수 있습니다.

themes.heading: 출입국 (in- en uitreis)
themes.text: 무비자 입국 가능 여부(1회 입국시 최대 연속 체류 가능 일자) ㅇ 외교관 여권 소지자 : O, (90일)일방 ㅇ 관용여권 소지자 : O, (90일)일방 ㅇ 일반여권 소지자(복수와 단수를 구분할 경우 별도 표기 필요) : X ㅇ 도착사증 발급 여부 : 도착사증 발급 - 최초 30일 부여, 1회 30일 추가 연장 가능 입국시 유의사항 등 ㅇ 미화 5천불 상당까지 소지 가능하며, 2만불 이상 소재시 사전 신고 필수 출국시 유의사항 등 ㅇ 도착사증 미발급 참고자료 ㅇ 해당 국가의 주한공관홈페이지 ㅇ 해당국가의 한국 대사관 홈페이지 : https://overseas.mofa.go.kr/tl-ko/index.do ㅇ 기타 참고자료 - 해당국 이민청 www.migracao.gov.tl - 세관 www.customs.gov.tl

themes.heading: 일반문화
themes.text: ㅇ 오전(본디아, Bondia), 오후(보아따르데, Boatarde), 저녁(본노이떼, Boanoite)인사 및 감사인사(오브리가도, Obrigado) 등의 현지 기본 인사말을 알아두시면 좋습니다. 여행시 수시로 인사말을 건네면, 대부분 인사말로 화답해 줍니다.

themes.heading: 종교관련
themes.text: ㅇ 종교의 자유가 있으며, 인구의 90%가 카톨릭이고 소수의 이슬람교, 기독교, 불교 등이 있습니다. 성당 방문 시에는 단정한 복장으로 방문하는 것이 좋으며, 소수 종교도 존중하시고 사원 방문 시 또한 단정한 복장으로 방문하시기 바랍니다.

themes.heading: 팁 문화
themes.text: ㅇ 동티모르는 팁 문화가 일반적이지 않고 호텔이나 식당 등의 계산서에 봉사료가 포함된 경우도 있으나, 외국인들은 일반적으로 봉사한 직원에게 소액을 지급하기도 합니다. ㅇ 일부 호텔이나 식당에서는 세금을 별도로 부과하기도 합니다.

themes.heading: 주요 교통 법규 및 문화
themes.text: ㅇ 자동차 운전석 위치가 한국과 반대입니다. (일본, 유럽과 동일) ㅇ 수도 및 지방도시에 신호체계 극히 드뭅니다. ㅇ 산악 지형이 국토의 대부분이며 도로 상태(비포장, 차선, 가로등) 열악합니다. - 수도 딜리 중심부 3km 반경 정도만 포장이 되어 있으며, 이외 수도를 포함한 대부분 도로 비포장 - 산악 지형이 국토의 70%이상을 차지하며 고갯길이 많으나 가드레일이 없습니다. - 수도 딜리 중심부를 제외하면 가로등 체계가 정비되어 있지 않습니다. - 도로망은 딜리를 중심으로 동서남북으로 개설돼 있으나 대부분이 산허리와 해안선을 끼고 개설된 폭이 좁은 2 차선 도로이며 80퍼센트 이상이 비포장 도로 입니다. 국토의 지형이 험준한 산맥으로 되어 있기 때문에 직선의 고속도로를 건설하는 것이 원천적으로 불가능하여, 우리나라의 각 산에 산불 방지를 목적으로 건설된 소방도로 상태보다도 떨어집니다. 딜리 시내를 제외한 지방의 대부분의 도로가 가파르고 커브가 심하며 깊이 페인 구덩이가 많은 비포장 상태이며, 4륜구동만이 운행 가능한 곳도 있습니다. - 차량등록증, 차량정비검사서 및 운전면허증을 항상 소지해야 합니다.(불심검문) ※ 한국운전면허증 및 국제운전면허증 인정되나 장기 체류시, 동티모르 운전면허증으로 교체 권장 ㅇ 대부분 도로와 인도간 구분이 없고, 인도가 없거나 매우 좁으며, 횡단보도 또한 부재하여 보행 환경 극히 열악합니다. - 운전시 행인과의 간격을 주의해야 합니다. - 신호등과 횡단보도 부재로 인해 무단횡단이 많기 때문에 주의해야 합니다. ㅇ 특히 운전중 교통사고 등으로 많은 사람들이 갑자기 몰려들기 때문에 만약 신변 위협을 느낄 경우 빨리 그 자리에서 벗어나 가까운 경찰서로 가서 신고하는 것이 안전합니다.

themes.heading: 관련 사건사고 사례
themes.text: ㅇ △낮은 교통법규 이해도 △오토바이 및 택시의 지나친 공격적 운전으로 인해 사고 발생률 높음 - ‘18년에도 당지 UN직원이 오토바이 사고 부상 ㅇ 경찰의 음주단속이 부재하여 음주운전으로 인한 사고 발생 빈번 ㅇ 연말연시 야간에는 현지인이 주변 차량에 돌을 던지거나 화살촉을 발사하여 사고를 유발, 현금을 요구하는 경우도 존재

themes.heading: 관련 사건사고 발생시 대처방법
themes.text: ㅇ 교통사고 발생시 경찰(112)에 신고 및 재외공관 긴급연락처로 연락하십시오. ㅇ 현지인이 차량을 따라오며 고의로 시비를 걸 경우, 정차하지 말고 현장을 벗어나 우리 대사관 또는 인근 경찰서로 이동해 도움을 요청

themes.heading: 대중교통
themes.text: ㅇ 대중교통 수단으로는 딜리 시내에서 운행되는 미크로렛이라 불리는 미니밴 버스가 있으나, 기종이 너무 노후화(안전하지 않음) 되었으며 에어컨도 없고 너무 비좁음은 물론, 일정한 터미널이나 정류소가 없고 운행 시간이 불규칙하며 오전 6시-저녁 7시경까지만 운행하기 때문에 외국인이 이용하기에는 불편한 점이 매우 많습니다. ㅇ 택시의 경우 대부분 노란색 차량이며, 택시를 잡기는 힘들지 않습니다. 택시는 미터기가 없으므로 탑승전 가격을 협상하여야 하고, 소액권을 준비하셔야 합니다. ※ 최근 미터기 택시 도입(소수차량) ㅇ 야간에 긴급히 사용해야할 경우에 대비하여, 택시 기사의 휴대폰 번호를 파악하고 있으면, 야간에도 콜이 가능합니다. 다만 대부분이 10년 이상이 된 노후 차량임은 물론 에어컨이 작동하지 않기 때문에 업무 상 쾌적하게 사용하기에는 부적합 합니다. 따라서 업무 수행 시에는 렌터카를 이용하는 것이 일반적이며 차종에 따라 다르지만 임대요금은 1일당 100~150불 내외입니다. ㅇ 딜리와 지방 사이를 운항하는 교통수단으로는 미니버스와 트럭이 있습니다. 트럭의 경우 화물 적재 공간에 의자를 설치하여 운행하며 요금은 미니버스의 절반 수준이나 안전상 매우 취약합니다.

themes.heading: 기타 유의사항
themes.text: ㅇ 지방 방문시 도로상태가 열악하고 차량정비소 및 주유소가 미비한 실정임을 감안, 여분의 장비(예비 타이어, 비상 유류 등) 준비를 권장합니다. ㅇ 특히 우천시 산사태로 인해 도로가 유실된 경우가 많으므로 지방 방문시 사전에 도로상황을 면밀히 확인해야 합니다.

themes.heading: 날씨
themes.text: ㅇ 연평균 기온 : 27℃∼30℃내외(열대와 아열대 기후의 중간정도) ㅇ 우기(11월∼5월), 건기(6월∼10월) ㅇ 지역에 따른 강수량 편차가 크며, 7월-8월은 아침, 저녁으로 선선한 편입니다.

themes.heading: 기타
themes.text: ㅇ 말라리아, 뎅기열, 일본뇌염, 간염, 결핵 등 발병률이 높으며, 특히 말라리아, 뎅기열에 감염될 위험이 아주 높은데 치료를 받을 수 있는 병원 시설이나 의사가 부족합니다. 증상이 심한 뎅기열 등의 감염 판정 시에는 호주 또는 인도네시아로 신속히 후송, 치료를 받는 것도 필요합니다. 주재국 체류 중 모기에 물리지 않도록 모든 예방조치를 취해야 하며, 건강한 몸 상태를 유지하여 모기에 물리더라도 발병하지 않도록 해야 합니다. ㅇ 주재국의 경우 병원내 의사 수가 부족하며 의료시설도 낙후되어 있어 중병의 경우 인근 싱가포르나 발리로 이동하여 치료를 받아야 함으로 비싼 치료비를 감안, 입국 이전에 비상시를 대비하여 반드시 여행자 보험에 가입하시기 바랍니다. ㅇ 물은 반드시 병에 든 생수(mineral water)를 구입해서 마셔야 하며, 식당에서 음료수에 넣어 주는 얼음도 가능한 피하는 것이 바람직합니다. 아울러 열대지방의 특성상, 생선회, 익히지 않은 채소 등 날것을 먹기보다는 삶거나, 튀기는 등 조리한 음식을 섭취하는 것이 위생상 안전하다고 하겠습니다.

themes.heading: Country summary
themes.text: There was no change to the advisory level or risk indicators. Advisory summary was updated. Exercise Increased Caution in Timor-Leste due to risk of unrest and crime. Read the entire Travel Advisory. Advisory summary Police in Timor-Leste sometimes use force and tear gas against protestors. Stone throwing attacks on vehicles can occur during gang conflicts and periods of unrest. Sex-based violence and sexual harassment are common in Timor-Leste. If you decide to travel to Timor-Leste: Enroll in the Smart Traveler Enrollment Program (STEP) to get important updates and alerts from the U.S. embassy or consulate. Enrolling helps the U.S. embassy or consulate contact you or your emergency contact in an emergency. Review the Country Security Report for Timor-Leste. Keep a low profile Always be aware of your personal safety and security. Use caution when walking or driving at night. Avoid demonstrations or crowds Keep travel documents up-to-date and easily accessible. Review our information on Crime Abroad and for Victims of Crime. Visit the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) website for the latest Travel Health Information for Timor-Leste. Whether you’re a first time or frequent traveler, use the International Travel Checklist. We highly recommend that you buy travel insurance before you travel. Check with your travel insurance provider about evacuation assistance, medical insurance, and trip cancelation coverage.

themes.heading: Reisehinweise (EDA)
themes.text: Timor-Leste Zuletzt editiert: 1/8/2026 11:49:21 AM Grundsätzliche Einschätzung Reisen nach Timor-Leste gelten grundsätzlich als sicher. Meiden Sie Demonstrationen jeder Art, denn Ausschreitungen sind möglich. Das Risiko von terroristischen Anschlägen kann auch in Timor-Leste nicht ausgeschlossen werden. Die Rubrik Terrorismus und Entführungen macht auf die Risiken des Terrorismus aufmerksam. Terrorismus und Entführungen Informieren Sie sich vor und während der Reise in den Medien über die Sicherheitslage. Befolgen Sie die Anweisungen der Behörden (Ausgangssperren usw.). Kriminalität Kleinkriminalität ist verbreitet und kann mitunter auch gewalttätige Züge annehmen. Gewalttätige Auseinandersetzungen zwischen Banden kommen vor. Beachten Sie unter anderem nachstehende Vorsichtsmassnahmen: Lassen Sie Ihren Aufenthalt vorzugsweise von einer ortskundigen Vertrauensperson organisieren und befolgen Sie deren Anweisungen. Reisen Sie möglichst nicht allein und ausschliesslich tagsüber. Frauen wird empfohlen, generell ihrer persönlichen Sicherheit grosse Aufmerksamkeit zu schenken. Verzichten Sie nach Einbruch der Dunkelheit auf Taxifahrten und benützen Sie tagsüber nur Taxis, die Sie oder Ihr Hotel vorgängig bestellt haben, oder die über eine offizielle Lizenz und einen Taxameter verfügen. Meiden Sie nach Einbruch der Dunkelheit unbeleuchtete Strassen sowie einsame Gegenden und verzichten Sie generell auf Spaziergänge. Tragen Sie keine Wertgegenstände (Uhren, Schmuck usw.) und nur wenig Geld auf sich. Tragen Sie immer Ihre Ausweispapiere und Ihr Mobiltelefon auf sich. Befolgen Sie die Anweisungen der lokalen Sicherheitskräfte. Betrugsversuche sind möglich. Vorsicht ist geboten, insbesondere bei unaufgeforderten kommerziellen oder technologischen Angeboten. Verkehr und Infrastruktur Es gilt Linksverkehr. Die Strassen sind oft in schlechtem Zustand. Das unvorhersehbare Verhalten vieler Verkehrsteilnehmenden bildet ein zusätzliches Risiko. Ausserhalb von Dili wird empfohlen, sich während der Regenzeit in einem Allradfahrzeug fortzubewegen. Von nächtlichen Überlandfahrten wird abgeraten. Infrastruktur und Verwaltung sind noch im Auf- und Ausbau begriffen. Ausserhalb von Dili ist die Infrastruktur für Reisende (Unterkünfte, Restaurants usw.) rar und meistens sehr bescheiden. Internetverbindungen sind im ganzen Land häufig langsam oder fallen zeitweise ganz aus, auch in Dili. Dies beeinträchtigt unter anderem Finanzdienstleistungen wie Bargeldabhebungen und Kartenzahlungen. Grenzübergänge können kurzfristig vorübergehend geschlossen werden, z.B. um die Ausbreitung von Krankheiten zu verhindern. Informieren Sie sich bei den lokalen Behörden oder direkt bei den Grenzposten. Besondere rechtliche Bestimmungen Vergehen gegen das Betäubungsmittelgesetz werden schon bei kleinen Mengen und bei jeder Art von Drogen mit langjährigen Gefängnisstrafen (bis lebenslänglich) geahndet. Die Haftbedingungen sind schwierig; die inhaftierten Personen müssen selbst für die Ernährung und medizinische Versorgung aufkommen. Naturbedingte Risiken Timor-Leste liegt in einer Erdbebenzone. Tsunami, die durch Vulkanausbrüche oder Erdbeben im pazifischen Raum ausgelöst werden, können auch Timor-Leste erreichen. Während der Regenzeit kann es zu Überschwemmungen, Schlammlawinen und Infrastrukturschäden kommen. Vereinzelt können Wirbelstürme (Zyklone) auftreten. Auch der Reiseverkehr kann vorübergehend beeinträchtigt werden. Weltorganisation für Meteorologie (WMO) Sollte sich während Ihres Aufenthalts eine Naturkatastrophe ereignen, melden Sie sich möglichst rasch bei Ihren Angehörigen und befolgen Sie die Anweisungen der Behörden. Medizinische Versorgung Die medizinische Versorgung ist in Dili nur beschränkt und ausserhalb der Hauptstadt nicht überall gewährleistet. Personen mit einem negativen Rhesusfaktor können Schwierigkeiten haben, eine Bluttransfusion zu erhalten. Ernsthafte Erkrankungen und Verletzungen müssen im Ausland (z.B. Singapur) behandelt werden. Krankenhäuser verlangen vor Behandlungen eine finanzielle Garantie (Kreditkarte oder Vorschusszahlung). Wenn Sie auf bestimmte Medikamente angewiesen sind, sollte Ihre Reiseapotheke einen ausreichenden Vorrat enthalten. Bedenken Sie jedoch: In vielen Ländern gelten besondere Vorschriften für die Mitnahme von betäubungsmittelhaltigen Medikamenten (z.B. Methadon) und Substanzen, mit denen psychische Erkrankungen behandelt werden. Erkundigen Sie sich gegebenenfalls vor der Abreise direkt bei der zuständigen ausländischen Vertretung (Botschaft oder Konsulat) und konsultieren Sie die Rubrik Reiselinks, wo Sie unter anderem weitere Informationen zu diesem Thema sowie generell zur Reisemedizin finden. Über die Verbreitung von Krankheiten und mögliche Schutzmassnahmen informieren medizinische Fachpersonen und Impfzentren. Reiselinks Besondere Hinweise Der US-Dollar gilt als offizielle Währung. Teilweise kann mit Kreditkarte bezahlt und Geld abgehoben werden. Nützliche Adressen Notrufnummer: 115 Zollvorschriften: Timor-Leste Customs Schweizer Vertretungen im Ausland: Wenn Sie im Ausland in eine Notlage geraten, können Sie sich an die nächste Schweizer Vertretung oder an die Helpline EDA wenden. Schweizerische Botschaft in Jakarta, Indonesien Helpline EDA Ausländische Vertretungen in der Schweiz: Auskunft über die Einreisevorschriften (zugelassene Ausweise, Visum etc.) erteilen die zuständigen ausländischen Botschaften und Konsulate. Sie informieren auch über die Zollbestimmungen für die Ein- und Ausfuhr von Tieren und Waren: elektronische Geräte, Souvenirs, Medikamente etc. Ausländische Vertretungen in der Schweiz Ausschluss der Haftung Ausschluss der Haftung Die Reisehinweise des Eidgenössischen Departements für auswärtige Angelegenheiten (EDA) stützen sich auf eigene, als vertrauenswürdig eingeschätzte Informationsquellen. Sie verstehen sich als nützliche Hinweise zur Planung einer Reise. Das EDA kann Reisenden aber den Entscheid und die Verantwortung für die Vorbereitung und Durchführung der Reise nicht abnehmen. Gefahrensituationen sind oft nicht vorhersehbar, unübersichtlich und können sich rasch ändern. Das EDA übernimmt keine Gewähr für die Vollständigkeit der Reisehinweise und für die Richtigkeit des Inhalts von verlinkten externen Internetseiten. Es lehnt jede Haftung für allfällige Schäden im Zusammenhang mit einer Reise ab. Forderungen im Zusammenhang mit der Annullierung einer Reise sind direkt beim Reisebüro oder der Reiseversicherung geltend zu machen.
